item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a solid scip concept. I think the writeup could be condensed and shortened; many of the details are thoughtful, but ultimately not very engaging. The Series I scips you reference in comments, the ones I remember most vividly, were very short. Strangely enough, your writeup for this SCP strikes me as far more realistic, in that it provides details that a containment specialist would want to review before engaging with the anomaly. My guess is that this realism weakened the piece; after all, most real-life containment procedures would be relatively boring documents.

The brief description of Foundation detective work was good worldbuilding. Unfortunately I think the dry, clinical tone restraint of the SCP format didn't serve that worldbuilding goal; it felt too detailed when I first read it, despite its brevity. After some reflection, I think the problem was actually a lack of space for narrative beats. I'd enjoy reading a tale that depicts the Foundation investigation with a little more vibrancy and drama.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Replaced "appendage analogous to a light bulb" with "appendage resembling a light bulb".

"Analogous" has a special meaning in biology (see Wikipedia). You could instead say something like:

"appendage analogous to a flower/flower bud/thorn"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the 'super cool plants' overlay suits this well

item.title: Junior Staff Post - Noncompliant Images
item.content:encoded: The images in this article have been removed for noncompliance with the site's license.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of THE most underrated SCP articles ive read to date.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: “mimetic” refers to something that demonstrates mimicry, so i believe it does fit in this case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I had an instance or two of Luxlosidae scola in my basement. Bugs got trapped in it all the damn time. I really liked this SCP, and it deserves more than a 39. The "mimetic" category should probably be fixed though. Just a tiny nitpick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thank you so much! i'm somewhat of a fan of yours so i am filled with gratitude.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
also, i would be interested to see evidence of actual plants that lack any chlorophyll. even carnivorous and parasitic plants rely on photosynthesis for respiratory substrates.

Beech drops spring to mind (a parasitic plant that has precisely zero chlorophyll). I can't think of any other examples off the top of my head, but I recall that there are a few that subsist on nutrients from fungi (in a one-way parasitic relationship).

this scp was genuinely supposed to a throwback to the piece of shit forgotten about scps from season one; an attempt to show that not all scps have to be some reality altering, end-of-the-world causing super human phenomenon. and as a result, it is boring and inconsistent.

This is an appreciable sentiment (and one many of my own skips share!), but you can still invoke that old-school season 1 feel of low-key anomalies without being inconsistent or boring.

That being said, I liked this skip — so hey! +1, regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
im glad you've mentioned this stuff actually. im always up for a science-y chat. the things you've mentioned are probably not explicitly clear due to my writing at the time (i'd like to think i've improved), however they are supposed to conjure up questions from those with a scientific background. the reference to metallic carbon relates to some papers i've read of attempts to develop carbon with metallic properties, of which allotropes are theoretically possible.

as you have said, keratin does not naturally exist in plants in quantities enough to create a tissue. however the fact that these organisms are made by amateur scientists in a house in the middle of nowhere lends credence that they were trying to work with experimental properties of their organism.

also, i would be interested to see evidence of actual plants that lack any chlorophyll. even carnivorous and parasitic plants rely on photosynthesis for respiratory substrates.

thank you for the comment though. this scp was genuinely supposed to a throwback to the piece of shit forgotten about scps from season one; an attempt to show that not all scps have to be some reality altering, end-of-the-world causing super human phenomenon. and as a result, it is boring and inconsistent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some science issues with this:

Metal ions integrated into these proteins…such as: gold, carbon, an unknown metallic alloy…

Carbon is not a metal, and I don't think you can have an ion of an alloy, which is a mixture of metals rather than a single element or molecule.

most SCP-3184 instances posses few to no chloroplasts within their cells. It is unclear how the process of photosynthesis is circumnavigated in SCP-3184 species

There are parasitic plants that operate just fine without chlorophyll. It may be reasonable to say that an anomalous, carnivorous plant gets enough nutrients from its prey that it doesn't require photosynthesis.

an exterior shell…which is keratinous in nature

I believe keratin is found exclusively in animals. I might suggest chitin, which is a comparable protein found in, among other things, the cell walls of fungi. Considering the re-structuring of the Kingdom classifications of organisms that seems to happen frequently in biology, I'm not even sure this really qualifies as a plant, though I'm not an expert to tell you what kingdom it might actually fall into.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this SCP, a very cool idea, even if similar ideas have been done before, like SCP-799. And the aforementioned SCP-193 & SCP-611. You still execute this idea wonderfully and make it feel a bit more unique with the multiple species and images to go along with them. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of those articles that click after just a couple sentences. The concept itself may not be new, but it's realized very well.
I liked it, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: oh gosh i didn't find those ones in my research. once again, i think im going to flesh out the final addendum to differentiate it a little from other nature channel-type scps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It just seems like one more case of a biological creature mimicking a manmade object. I'm not sure what this does better or differently than SCP-193 or SCP-611. It's well written and has a good tone, but the actual content is just really long and predictable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: im inclined to agree. i think id like to stick with the idea that they were manufactured as that has been something thats been part of the scp since its inception, however i do think i could add to it a wee more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting and well-developed little thing, sort of a throwback to articles of several years ago. The only thing I don't care for is the very last addendum. I feel like the article is better if these have a more inexplicable origin, and there really isn't much more information in the last part other than "someone's responsible, and we can't find them."
